title: What Became of the Chimps Who Revolutionized Our Understanding of Animal Behavior?
gist: A new European Psychologist paper by Javier Virués-Ortega reconstructs, via archival detective work, what happened to Wolfgang Köhler's Tenerife chimpanzee colony (led by Sultan) after it was moved to a Berlin zoo, filling in a history the founding texts of comparative psychology left out.

insights.claim: Sultan, a male chimpanzee captured as a juvenile in South Cameroon in 1912 along with five wild females, was held on Tenerife until 1920, where research led by German psychologist Wolfgang Köhler founded the field of comparative psychology.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A single captive colony's research program can define an entire scientific discipline's founding methodology.
insights.locator: If it weren't for Sultan and his primate clan

themes.theme: Rediscovering the animals behind the science

insights.claim: A new paper in the journal European Psychologist, led by University of Auckland psychologist Javier Virués-Ortega, used archival detective work — newspapers, photos, zoo records, letters, memoirs, museum specimens, public records, and interviews — to trace what happened to Köhler's famous Tenerife chimps after they were moved to a Berlin zoo.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Illustrates how historical/archival methods can recover overlooked animal-welfare narratives buried inside canonical science history.
insights.locator: A recent paper published in the journal European Psychologist

insights.claim: Researcher Javier Virués-Ortega frames the project as deliberately correcting a gap in the textbook record: the famous Tenerife chimps are remembered for what they revealed about the mind, but not for what became of the animals themselves.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A reminder that landmark scientific case studies often omit the fate of their subjects — a recurring blind spot worth watching for in other 'foundational' research narratives.
insights.quote: These chimpanzees are usually remembered for what they revealed about the mind. We wanted to ask the historical question textbooks mostly leave out: What happened to the animals themselves?
insights.locator: said Virués-Ortega in a press release
